_meta.purpose: Curated grounding dataset for Ingredient Intel. Used to verify food/beverage additive claims against official regulatory bodies instead of relying purely on LLM recall.
_meta.coverage: Starter seed set (~28 of the most commonly seen food additives). Not exhaustive — extend as needed.
_meta.disclaimer: Compiled from publicly available regulatory sources (JECFA/WHO-FAO, EFSA/EU, US FDA, FSSAI India, IARC/WHO). Regulatory status can change — verify against the primary source link before relying on this for anything beyond a hackathon/demo context. ADI values are approximate and rounded from primary sources.

bodies.JECFA: Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives — sets international Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI) values
bodies.EFSA_EU: European Food Safety Authority / EU Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 on food additives
bodies.FDA: US Food and Drug Administration — 21 CFR food additive / GRAS regulations
bodies.FSSAI: Food Safety and Standards Authority of India — FSS (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011
bodies.IARC: International Agency for Research on Cancer (WHO) — carcinogen hazard classification, Groups 1 / 2A / 2B / 3

regulatory.JECFA: ADI established, approx. 0–5 mg/kg body weight/day (as benzoic acid)
regulatory.EFSA_EU: Approved under Reg. (EC) 1333/2008; re-evaluated by EFSA (2016), approval maintained with usage limits
regulatory.FSSAI: Permitted preservative with category-specific maximum limits under FSS (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011

additives.knownConcerns: Can react with ascorbic acid (vitamin C) under heat, light, or storage to form trace benzene — mainly relevant in soft drinks combining the two. Regulators continue to permit it at approved levels.

regulatory.JECFA: ADI 'not specified' (i.e. no safety concern at typical use levels)

additives.knownConcerns: Generally regarded as one of the milder preservatives; rare contact sensitivity reported in cosmetics use.

regulatory.JECFA: ADI 'not specified' (no safety concern at normal dietary levels)

additives.knownConcerns: Naturally occurring in citrus fruit; can contribute to dental enamel erosion in high-acid beverages, but not a toxicological concern.

regulatory.EFSA_EU: Approved only for specific cured-meat categories with strict maximum residual limits
regulatory.FDA: Approved for meat curing under USDA/FDA rules, with maximum permitted levels
regulatory.FSSAI: Permitted in cured/processed meat categories only, with limits
regulatory.IARC: Processed meat (the product category, not the additive itself) is IARC Group 1; nitrite/nitrate compounds themselves are not separately classified by IARC

additives.knownConcerns: Can form N-nitroso compounds (some carcinogenic) when combined with amines, especially under high-heat cooking. This is the primary driver behind IARC's classification of processed meat as Group 1 (carcinogenic to humans) as a category.

regulatory.JECFA: ADI 'not specified' — JECFA has repeatedly found no safety concern at normal dietary intake
regulatory.EFSA_EU: Approved; EFSA re-evaluation (2017) maintained approval with a group ADI for glutamates

additives.knownConcerns: Long-rumored 'MSG symptom complex' (headache, flushing) has not been substantiated in controlled studies; major food-safety bodies consider it safe for the general population at typical intake.

regulatory.JECFA: ADI reaffirmed July 2023 at 0–40 mg/kg bw/day; JECFA concluded no convincing evidence of adverse effects at this intake
regulatory.FDA: Approved food additive, ADI 50 mg/kg bw/day (US figure differs slightly from JECFA/EFSA)
regulatory.IARC: Classified Group 2B — 'possibly carcinogenic to humans' (July 2023), based on what IARC itself described as limited evidence

additives.knownConcerns: This is the textbook case of 'hazard vs. risk': IARC's 2B hazard classification (limited evidence) was issued the same week JECFA reaffirmed the ADI is unchanged — i.e. the risk assessors did not lower the safe-intake limit. A 70kg adult would need ~9-14 cans of diet soda/day to exceed the ADI.
No established human carcinogenicity or toxicity concern at approved levels; not metabolised by the body and excreted unchanged.
Largely passes through the body unmetabolised; some emerging research on gut-microbiome effects at high intakes is still being evaluated, not yet a basis for regulatory action.

regulatory.FDA: Approved (removed from US carcinogen list in 2000 after review of rat-specific mechanism)
regulatory.IARC: Previously listed, but delisted from IARC carcinogen classifications after mechanism shown to be rat-specific and not relevant to humans

additives.knownConcerns: Historical bladder-tumor link in rats was later shown to be due to a rat-specific urinary mechanism not applicable to humans; major agencies now consider it safe within ADI.

regulatory.JECFA: Previously had no ADI limitation; under continued review
regulatory.EFSA_EU: BANNED as a food additive across the EU since 7 August 2022 (Regulation (EU) 2022/63) — EFSA could not rule out genotoxicity concerns and could not confirm an ADI
regulatory.FDA: Still an approved color additive in US food as of early 2026; a 2023 citizen petition asking FDA to revoke this is pending, no ban issued
regulatory.FSSAI: Permitted in India as of current FSSAI rules — not aligned with the EU ban
regulatory.IARC: Powder form previously classified by EU/ECHA as a suspected inhalation carcinogen (Category 2); an EU Court of Justice ruling (Aug 2025) overturned that specific classification

additives.knownConcerns: This is a genuinely split-jurisdiction case: banned in EU food, still permitted in the US and India. Good example for your app to show 'this ingredient's status literally depends on which country's rules you apply.'

regulatory.JECFA: Evaluated; ADI previously established but use is now restricted/banned in major markets
regulatory.EFSA_EU: Already banned for food use in the EU, except limited use in certain cherries
regulatory.FDA: BANNED in US food and ingested drugs as of FDA order dated 15 Jan 2025 (Delaney Clause — found to cause cancer in male rats); food manufacturers have until 15 Jan 2027 to reformulate. Was already banned in US cosmetics since 1990
regulatory.FSSAI: Status varies; check current FSSAI colour-additive list for India-specific permission

additives.knownConcerns: FDA was explicit that the cancer mechanism seen in male rats (thyroid tumors via a hormone pathway) has not been observed in humans — the ban was triggered by the Delaney Clause's strict 'any carcinogen in animals' rule, not by direct human evidence.

regulatory.EFSA_EU: Approved, but EU requires a warning label ('may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children') under Reg. 1333/2008 Annex V
regulatory.FDA: Currently approved; included in HHS/FDA's June 2025 announced plan to phase out petroleum-based synthetic dyes by Oct 2027 (not yet banned as of early 2026)

additives.knownConcerns: Most widely used synthetic food dye in the US; associated in some studies with hyperactivity/attention effects in sensitive children (evidence is mixed) — this is the basis of the EU mandatory warning label.

regulatory.EFSA_EU: Approved, with mandatory 'may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children' warning label
regulatory.FDA: Currently approved; part of FDA/HHS's 2025-announced phase-out plan (target Oct 2027)

additives.knownConcerns: Known allergen/intolerance trigger in a small subset of people, especially those with aspirin sensitivity; linked in some studies to hyperactivity in children.

regulatory.JECFA: ADI approx. 0–0.3 mg/kg bw/day for BHA; similar order for BHT
regulatory.EFSA_EU: Approved with usage limits; under periodic re-evaluation
regulatory.IARC: BHA classified IARC Group 2B ('possibly carcinogenic to humans'); BHT not classified by IARC

additives.knownConcerns: BHA's 2B classification is based on animal studies (forestomach tumors in rodents, an organ humans don't have an exact equivalent of) — regulators have kept it approved at current use levels, but several manufacturers have voluntarily phased it out.

regulatory.JECFA: Could not establish an ADI due to genotoxicity/carcinogenicity concerns
regulatory.EFSA_EU: BANNED as a food additive in the EU/UK
regulatory.FDA: Still technically permitted in the US (most large bakeries voluntarily stopped using it); California's Food Safety Act bans it from 1 Jan 2027
regulatory.FSSAI: BANNED in India as a flour treatment agent (FSSAI notification, 2016) on cancer-risk grounds
regulatory.IARC: Classified IARC Group 2B ('possibly carcinogenic to humans')

additives.knownConcerns: Genuinely contested across jurisdictions: banned outright in the EU, UK, Canada, Brazil and India, but not federally banned in the US (California is the first US state to ban it, effective 2027).

Derived from red seaweed. Some animal/in-vitro studies link degraded carrageenan (poligeenan, a different substance not used in food) to gut inflammation; food-grade carrageenan itself has not been shown to cause this in regulatory reviews, though it remains a topic of ongoing research and some consumer concern.

regulatory.JECFA: Not a single defined additive (a sugar mixture); not subject to ADI
regulatory.EFSA_EU: No specific additive restriction — regulated as a food/sugar ingredient, not a food additive
regulatory.FSSAI: No specific additive restriction — regulated under sugar/sweetener standards

additives.knownConcerns: Not regulated as an 'additive' with a safety limit because it's a caloric sugar, not a functional additive — the health debate (excess fructose, metabolic effects) is a nutrition/dietary-pattern issue, not a food-safety/toxicology one, and should be framed that way rather than as an 'unsafe additive'.

regulatory.JECFA: WHO has called for global elimination of industrially-produced trans fat by 2023 target (REPLACE initiative)
regulatory.EFSA_EU: Industrial trans fat capped at 2g per 100g of fat EU-wide since April 2021 (Regulation (EU) 2019/649)
regulatory.FDA: Partially hydrogenated oils are NO LONGER 'Generally Recognized as Safe' in the US — FDA revoked GRAS status, final compliance June 2018
regulatory.FSSAI: Trans-fat content in food capped at 2% by FSSAI regulation (phased in through 2022)

additives.knownConcerns: One of the few ingredients with near-universal regulatory consensus against it — strongly linked to cardiovascular disease risk. If you see this on a label, it's a legitimate red flag almost everywhere.

regulatory.EFSA_EU: Approved with limits; mandatory allergen labelling above 10mg/kg or 10mg/L
regulatory.FSSAI: Permitted with limits and labelling requirements

additives.knownConcerns: Recognized allergen — can trigger asthma/anaphylaxis-like reactions in sulfite-sensitive individuals. This is a labelling/allergen issue more than a general-population toxicity issue.
Naturally derived (commonly from soy or sunflower); relevant mainly for soy-allergy labelling, not a general safety concern.

additives.displayName: Mono- and Diglycerides of Fatty Acids
additives.knownConcerns: Generally considered low-risk; may be derived from animal or plant fat sources, relevant for vegetarian/vegan and religious dietary flags rather than toxicology.

Often used alongside MSG to amplify umami; not linked to any established toxicity concern at normal dietary levels, but a relevant flag for people who specifically avoid glutamate/nucleotide flavour enhancers.
Naturally derived from guar beans; generally well-tolerated, though high intakes can have a laxative/bloating effect in sensitive individuals.
Naturally occurring pro-vitamin A pigment; no meaningful safety concern at food-use levels.
A nutrient as well as an additive; only relevant flag is the benzene-formation interaction with sodium benzoate (E211) noted above, not a concern on its own.
